coredata.dc:title: Promoted photocatalytic degradation and detoxication performance for norfloxacin on Z-scheme phosphate-doped BiVO4/graphene quantum dots/P-doped g-C3N4
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Meiyang
Yu, Han
Wang, Pan
Chi, Zexu
Zhang, Zhenzong
Dong, Binbin
Dong, Heng
Yu, Kai
Yu, Hongbing
coredata.dc:description:
A novel kind of Z-scheme ternary heterojunctions phosphate-doped BiVO4/graphene quantum dots/P-doped g-C3N4 (BVP/GQDs/PCN) were fabricated for the visible light degradation of norfloxacin (NOR), a typical antibiotic. Compared with binary type-II heterojunction phosphate-doped BiVO4/PCN (BVP/PCN), Z-scheme BVP/GQDs/PCN exhibited promoted interfacial charge transfer efficiency and broadened visible light response range, endowing them with excellent photodegradation activity and mineralization ability in NOR degradation. A high NOR degradation rate of 86.3% with a removal rate of total organic carbon (TOC) of 55.8% can be achieved over BVP/GQDs/PCN for 120 min visible light irradiation, which is an excellent performance compared with ever reported similar photocatalysts. In particular, because of the enhanced redox ability of photogenerated charges and the generation of multiple active species (eg.
OH and
O2 −) over Z-scheme photocatalytic system, the accumulation of highly toxic degradation intermediates was greatly inhibited, and a better detoxication performance was obtained compared to PCN and BVP/PCN. This work may shed light on the inhibition of highly toxic degradation intermediates of antibiotics by regulating the charge transfer mechanism, photocatalytic active species, and the degradation pathway of antibiotics.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel kind of Z-scheme ternary heterojunctions phosphate-doped BiVO4/graphene quantum dots/P-doped g-C3N4 (BVP/GQDs/PCN) were fabricated for the visible light degradation of norfloxacin (NOR), a typical antibiotic. Compared with binary type-II heterojunction phosphate-doped BiVO4/PCN (BVP/PCN), Z-scheme BVP/GQDs/PCN exhibited promoted interfacial charge transfer efficiency and broadened visible light response range, endowing them with excellent photodegradation activity and mineralization ability in NOR degradation. A high NOR degradation rate of 86.3% with a removal rate of total organic carbon (TOC) of 55.8% can be achieved over BVP/GQDs/PCN for 120 min visible light irradiation, which is an excellent performance compared with ever reported similar photocatalysts. In particular, because of the enhanced redox ability of photogenerated charges and the generation of multiple active species (eg.
OH and
O2 −) over Z-scheme photocatalytic system, the accumulation of highly toxic degradation intermediates was greatly inhibited, and a better detoxication performance was obtained compared to PCN and BVP/PCN. This work may shed light on the inhibition of highly toxic degradation intermediates of antibiotics by regulating the charge transfer mechanism, photocatalytic active species, and the degradation pathway of antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, the presence of antibiotics, a typical class of persistent organic pollutants, in water has attracted worldwide attention because of the unknown environmental impacts and possible damages to the botany and fauna in aquatic system [1]. Among antibiotics, norfloxacin (NOR) is one of the most widely used quinolone, which can cause serious damage to human health [2]. Consequently, a large number of strategies have been developed for the degradation of NOR, including chemical oxidation [3], photocatalysis [4,5], biological treatment [6], and electrolysis [7]. Among these methods, semiconductor photocatalysis has been proven to be an emerging and sustainable technology to remove NOR from water in the future [8]. Some traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, such as TiO2 [9] and ZnO [10], have been studied for photocatalytic degradation of NOR. However, they always suffer from some problems such as wide bandgap, insufficient charge-separation ability and inefficient capability of harvesting visible light, limiting their application. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop novel photocatalyst systems with higher efficiency, sustainability and stable visible-light-activity.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as an intriguing metal-free polymeric semiconductor, has been extensively applied in various fields of solar energy conversion and environmental remediation due to its highly thermal and chemical stability, good biocompatibility, “earth-abundant” nature, and tunable electronic structure [11-13]. In the last decade, intense efforts have been dedicated to improving the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. Non-metal doping of g-C3N4, such as S [14], F [15], and P [16], can narrow the bandgap of g-C3N4 and enhance its light harvesting capability. Exfoliating two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets from bulk g-C3N4 can obviously improve their charge migration and separation efficiencies [17-20]. Through the construction of porous nanostructured, for example three-dimensionally ordered macroporous g-C3N4 [21], light harvesting efficiency and mass transfer ability of photocatalysts can be notably enhanced [22].

Although promising efficiencies of g-C3N4 on water splitting, CO2 fixation and organic synthesis have been extensively reported, the photocatalytic activity of pure g-C3N4 in environmental remediation is still unsatisfied, due mainly to the weak photocatalytic oxidation capacity of pure g-C3N4that is relative to its lower valence band (VB) potential, which is insufficient to oxidize water (H2O) to produce the hydroxyl radical (
OH). In particular, for the degradation of antibiotics, the weak photocatalytic oxidation capacity of g-C3N4 would lead to the low mineralization degree of pollutants and the formation of highly toxic degradation intermediates. Therefore, coupling g-C3N4 with a narrow band-gap semiconductor and high VB potential to fabricate a Z-scheme photocatalytic system is an attractive strategy as it endows efficient charge separation and enhanced redox ability of photogenerated electrons and holes [23,24]. In this perspective, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) nanosheets should be a promising candidate to fabricate 2D/2D interface with g-C3N4 nanosheets. To further promote the interfacial charge transfer between g-C3N4 and BiVO4, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an emerging zero-dimensional carbon material with good conductivity and charge storage capacity [25], were employed as electron transfer mediators to construct a ternary Z-scheme system. Despite the fact that some Z-scheme photocatalytic systems based on g-C3N4 and BiVO4 had been reported [26-30], the influences of charge transfer mechanism of heterojunctions on the degradation pathway of antibiotics and the formation of highly toxic degradation intermediates are rarely discussed.

In the present work, a series of phosphate-doped BiVO4/GQDs/P doped g-C3N4 (BVP/GQDs/PCN) ternary Z-scheme photocatalysts were fabricated. Zero-dimensional GQDs deposited onto the surface and 2D/2D interface between phosphate-doped BiVO4 (BVP) and P doped g-C3N4 (PCN) nanosheets can not only act as electron mediators to promote the separation of photogenerated carriers and Z-scheme charge transfer via 2D/2D interface, but also extend the spectral response range of the photocatalysts. Notably high photocatalytic activities of BVP/GQDs/PCN in visible light photocatalytic degradation of NOR were achieved. In particular, compared with pristine g-C3N4 and binary BVP/PCN counterpart, Z-scheme BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalysts exhibited higher redox potentials, resulting in the promoted mineralization capacity. Meanwhile, the accumulation of highly toxic degradation intermediates was significantly restrained, as determined by HPLC-MS detection of reaction solutions. This work may provide a reference for the fabrication of photocatalysts with promoted detoxication performance for photodegradation of antibiotics.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), melamine (C3N3(NH2)3), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (ADP, NH4H2PO4), glacial acetic acid, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), dimethyl formamide (DMF), monolayer graphene oxide (mGO) and NOR are all analytically grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reahent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification.

The morphologies of the prepared samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G20). The actual doping level of P in PCN and Bi leaching were determined by ICP-OES/MS (Aglient 5110). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area tests were doing on an ASAP 2460 surface area analyzer. The crystal structures of the as-synthesized samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu Kα, Rigaku Ulitma IV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on Nicolet iS50 using the standard KBr disk method. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to determine the chemical states of elements using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. The diffuse reflectance UV–vis (DR UV–vis) spectra were measured by Shimadzu UV-2600 with an integrating sphere for the diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) and time-resolved fluorescence emission decay spectra (TR-PL) were examined by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. The identification of intermediate products formed during photodegradation were determined by HPLC-MS (Waters, USA). The mobile phase was composed of two solutions: solvent A was acetonitrile containing 0.1% formic acid and solvent B was 1.0% formic acid. The flow rate of mobile phase was 0.3 μL min−1. The electron spin resonance (ESR) was adopted to determine the existence of
OH and
O2 − radicals by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as scavenger. In detail, 5.0 mg of photocatalyst was added into 10 mL of deionized water or methanol, followed by the addition of 50.0 μL of DMPO with 4 min irradiation of visible light.
The synthesis procedure of BVP/GQDs/PCN ternary photocatalysts is depicted in Scheme 1 .

Bulk PCN was prepared via a one-pot method by heating the mixture of melamine and ADP. Specifically, 3.0 g of melamine and 0.35 g of ADP were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water in turn with ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. After a stir of 12 h at room temperature, the mixture was heated at 80 °C to remove water. The obtained solid product was totally dried at 100 °C for 6 h in oven. Then it was annealed at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 2.5 °C min−1 under air condition. The actual doping level of P in PCN was determined as 4.28 wt% by ICP-OES. For comparison, bulk g-C3N4 was prepared following the same procedure mentioned above in the absence of ADP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exfoliation of bulk PCN and modification of mGO
ce:section.ce:para: 75 mg of mGO was dispersed into 20 mL of DMF with ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and then heated in an autoclave at 180 °C for 5 h. The solution was diluted and transferred to a dialysis bag in deionized water to obtain RGO solution. Then 0.75 g of bulk PCN was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h, affording the exfoliated PCN. 0.75 g of exfoliated PCN was added into the RGO dispersion and the mixture was ultrasonically treated for 0.5 h. After stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the obtained mixture was heated at 80 °C to remove water. The obtained solid product was dried at 60 °C for 6 h in oven that were designated as RGO/PCN.

A certain amount of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 15 mL of glacial acetic acid. The same amount of NH4VO4 and 0.5 g of RGO/PCN were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 0.5 h. Then the two mixtures were transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) under vigorous stirring, followed by standing at 140 °C for 12 h. The precipitates were centrifuged and washed repeatedly with alcohol and deionized water. The obtained samples were designated as 1BVP/GQDs/PCN, 2BVP/GQDs/PCN, and 3BVP/GQDs/PCN, respectively, based on the different dosage of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and NH4VO4 (1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 mmol). If not specified, BVP/GQDs/PCN, which appears in the paper, represents 2BVP/GQDs/PCN. For comparison, pure BiVO4was prepared using the same procedure but without the addition of RGO/PCN. BVP/PCN was prepared using PCN rather than RGO/PCN.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated by the removal of NOR under visible light irradiation. A 300 W Xe lamp was employed as the light source with a 420 nm cut-off filter. In each batch of tests, 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in an aqueous solution of NOR (50 mL, 20 mg/L). Before light irradiation, the suspension was stirred in dark for 30 min to ensure the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and NOR. During the illumination, an aliquot (3 mL) was sampled at certain time intervals, and then filtrated through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the residual particles. The corresponding concentration of NOR was determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer with the absorbance at the characteristic band of 273 nm. To evaluate the stability of photocatalysts, four cycle tests were carried out under the same conditions. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was recovered by centrifugation, thoroughly washed by deionized water, dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and subjected to the next cycle.

The photocurrent spectra were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660B) equipped with a standard three-electrode system, where the sample, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl were used as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. 0.05 mol/L of Na2SO4 was used as electrolyte. To prepare the working electrode, 10 mg of sample was dispersed into 1 mL of ethyl alcohol with ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The suspension was dip-coated onto a 1 × 4 cm2 indium tin oxide glass electrode and then was dried at 60 °C for 30 min.

The toxicity of NOR and degradation intermediates was evaluated through bioluminescence inhibition assay, using V. fischeri as the target organism. 1 mL of the sample was acquired at certain time intervals and added with 0.1 mL of osmotic pressure regulating buffer. Then, the mixture was transformed into a sterilized flask containing V. fischeri suspension. After exposure for 15 min at 20 °C, the luminescence of V. fischeri was detected using a water toxicity analyzer. INH (luminescence inhibition) was determined as follows:
INH (%) = 100% − ITt / ICt ◊ 100%
where ITt and ICt were the luminescence values of the sample and deionized water, respectively, at time t.

To investigate the morphology and microstructure of the samples, SEM and TEM images were collected. The PCN showed a micron scale bulk morphology with wrinkled surface (Fig. S1a). The mGO exhibited a monolayer structure as shown in Fig. S1b. The SEM images of BVP/GQDs/PCN ternary heterojunction are shown in Fig. 1 a. It can be found that the BVP nanoplates with a large lateral size of 0.5–1.0 μm and a thickness of 50–100 nm were partially wrapped by PCN nanosheets. However, as shown in Fig. S1c, the pure BiVO4 exhibited a spherical morphology with a diameter of ∼1.0 μm agglomerated by nanoplates. The change of morphology of BiVO4 can be attributed to the dissolution of phosphate in the solution, acting as a structure-directing reagent, which plays a key role in the nucleation and growth process of BiVO4 nanoplates [31].

The ternary heterojunction structure of BVP/GQDs/PCN can be also clearly observed from TEM image (Fig. 1b) and corresponding EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 1e). The submicron sized BVP nanoplates can be seen to deposit on the surface of PCN nanosheets. And P element was evenly dispersed in PCN and BiVO4 (Fig. 1e), suggesting the successful doping of P in g-C3N4 and phosphate was doped into BiVO4 during the synthesis of BVP/GQDs/PCN, respectively. In addition, from the HRTEM images of BVP/GQDs/PCN (Fig. 1c), some nanoparticles with a diameter of ∼5 nm can be observed on the surface of PCN and the marked lattice distance of 0.34 nm is attributed to the (002) plane of GQDs, which can be ascribed to the formation of GQDs by reducing in DMF and further cutting off under hydrothermal conditions [32]. To prove that, the pure GQDs was synthesized from mGO under the same reaction conditions, and the TEM and HRTEM images are shown in Fig. S2. It can be found that the obtained GQDs had a relatively uniform diameter of about 5 nm and the marked lattice distance of 0.34 nm can be ascribed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [33]. In Fig. 1d, two crystal facets with the lattice spacing of 0.34 and 0.20 nm are identified and a crossing angle of 72°, which coincide well with the (002) and (101) planes of GQDs, respectively. In addition, the marked lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.25 nm in Fig. 1d can be ascribed to the (002) plane of BiVO4 [34,35]. The elemental mapping images (Fig. 1e) clearly revealed that C, N, P, Bi, V and O elements were all presented. The SEM and TEM characterization indicated the successful construction of ternary heterojunction.

Fig. 2 a, b and Fig. S3a shows XRD patterns of as-prepared samples. The two diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.4° were assigned to the (100) and (002) planes of bulk g-C3N4, corresponding to the interlayer packing and interplanar stacking reflections of conjugated aromatic systems of g-C3N4, respectively. After P doping, the crystalline structure of g-C3N4 was well preserved, and only a slight decrease of peak intensity can be observed. However, compared with PCN, the peak intensity of RGO/PCN obviously decreased because of the exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4 to multilayered nanosheets [18]. No diffraction peaks assigned to GO or RGO were observed in the XRD pattern of BVP/GQDs/PCN due to the low content of RGO or high dispersion of GQDs in the material [36]. It can be also observed that the characteristic diffraction peaks of monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS NO. 14-0688) and g-C3N4 were well preserved in BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst without any impurity diffraction peaks related to BiPO4, Bi2O3 or V2O5 [37]. In Fig. 2b, the magnified view of (121) and (040) peaks shift towards lower angles could be due to the doping phosphate into monoclinic BiVO4 [38]. These results indicate that phosphate has been well inserted into BiVO4 lattice without forming any segregated impurity phase. As shown in Fig. S3a, it can be seen that all the BVP/GQDs/PCN heterojunction show similar peak signals, confirming the successful synthesis of ternary heterojunction.

The composition and functional groups of the samples can be also confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2c, the peaks located at 1244, 1319, 1412, 1560 and 1637 cm−1 can be ascribed to the typical stretching vibration modes of g-C3N4 heterocycles. The peak at 808 cm−1 belonged to the characteristic breathing mode of triazine units [39]. The absorption band at 727 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibration band of V-O group of BiVO4 [40]. The spectrum of PCN was similar to that of g-C3N4, which proved that the typical graphitic structure of carbon nitride was well maintained even after doping of P heteroatom. No peaks corresponding to P-related functional groups were observed, which may be owing to the overlap of the bands with those of strong CN vibrations or the small amount of P in the samples [41]. The main peaks of BVP and PCN can also be found in the FT-IR spectrum of BVP/GQDs/PCN, which indicated the successful synthesis of the composites, in agreement with the results of SEM, TEM, and XRD characterizations.

DR UV–vis spectra were recorded to evaluate the optical response of the photocatalysts, and the band gap energies of the samples were estimated based on the plots of (αhν)2 vs. hν. As shown in Fig. 2d, after P doping, the band gap of PCN (2.64 eV) slightly decreased compared with that of g-C3N4 (2.66 eV), suggesting the emergence of impurity states in the band gap. As shown in Fig. S3b, all the BVP/GQDs/PCN heterojunction exhibited obviously increased visible-light responsibility, especially in the wavelength region from 550 to 800 nm compared with unitary PCN and BiVO4, and binary BVP/PCN and RGO/PCN, which can be attributed to the formation of GQDs with quantum confinement effect [42].

The BET surface areas of g-C3N4, PCN, RGO/PCN, BiVO4 and BVP/GQDs/PCN determined by N2 sorption experiments are listed in Table S1. It can be seen that the addition of P can introduce a lot of mesopores (10–50 nm). Compared with g-C3N4, PCN showed a larger BET surface area (10.35 m2 g−1), which may be attributed to the decreased polymerization degree after P doping [43]. RGO/PCN exhibited a higher BET surface area than bulk PCN, suggesting the successful exfoliation of bulk PCN to nanosheets. The increased specific surface area was likely to favor adsorption of pollutants.

The surface chemical composition and valence states of the samples were investigated by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of PCN (Fig. 3 a) clearly indicates the existence of C, N, P and O elements, suggesting the successful doping of P in g-C3N4. The binding energy peaks of C, N, P, O, Bi and V can be observed in the XPS survey spectrum of BVP/GQDs/PCN. For C 1s spectra of g-C3N4 and PCN (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 284.6 eV and ∼288.0 eV can be assigned to CC bonds and the sp2-bonded carbon (NCN), respectively [44]. A new peak at 286.1 eV corresponding to CO bond was observed from C 1s spectrum of BVP/GQDs/PCN, which can be attributed to the oxygen-containing groups on edge sites of GQDs and should be beneficial to the coupling of GQDs with BVP nanocrystals [45]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the three deconvoluted N 1s peaks at 398.6, 399.5, and 400.9 eV were corresponded to the CNC group of sp2 N in triazine rings, ternary nitrogen groups (NC3), and amino functional groups (CNH) of g-C3N4, respectively [46]. After P doping, the peaks of CNC and NC3 shift to the lower binding energies, indicating that the chemical state of hybridized aromatic N changed after P modification. The intensity of CNC peak in PCN was obviously decreased compared to g-C3N4 due to the replacement of CNC bonds by PN bonds. This can be further confirmed by P 2p XPS spectrum of PCN (Fig. 3d), in which the peak at 133.8 eV can be associated with the PN bonds [47]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3e and 3f, the characteristic V 2p and Bi 4f XPS peaks of BiVO4 can be observed in XPS spectrum of BVP/GQDs/PCN, but slightly shifting to a higher binding energy (ca. 0.1 eV), indicating phosphate dopants do not affect the chemical states of Bi and V but alter the chemical environments of them [48]. The results of XPS characterization further confirmed the formation of BVP/GQDs/PCN ternary heterojunction and the occurrence of interfacial charge transfer.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized photocatalysts was evaluated by the NOR degradation efficiency under visible-light irradiation. Fig. S4 displays adsorption ability of the photocatalysts in the dark condition. It can be found that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the catalysts and NOR can be reached within 30 min. Hence, 30 min of dark adsorption was applied to all the photocatalytic experiments before visible light irradiation. Fig. 4 a shows the variation of the NOR degradation efficiency as irradiation time over the as-prepared samples. The NOR degradation efficiencies within 120 min visible light irradiation follow a decreasing order as 2BVP/GQDs/PCN (86.3%) > 3BVP/GQDs/PCN (72.4%) > 1BVP/GQDs/PCN (65.4%) > BVP/PCN (47.2%) > BiVO4/GQDs (40.4%) > BiVO4 (37.1%) > PCN (23.2%) > g-C3N4 (12.7%). The degradation efficiency of NOR on pure g-C3N4 was nearly doubled by P-doping, which can be attributed to the narrowed band gap and increased light response capacity. Moreover, the NOR degradation efficiency over BVP/PCN reached 47.2%, which is almost two times higher than that of PCN, and also obviously higher than that of BiVO4. After introducing GQDs, the degradation efficiency of NOR further increased to 86.3%, suggesting the successful construction of ternary heterojunction. Among these ternary photocatalysts, 2BVP/GQDs/PCN, with a moderate loading amount of BVP, exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity.

From the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S5), it can be seen that the relative intensities of the two characteristic peaks of NOR were decreased gradually and the peak at 273 nm was shifted to the left obviously as the light irradiation time was prolonged, indicating the partial mineralization of NOR under this reaction system. As shown in Fig. 4b, the total organic carbon (TOC) removal rates over these photocatalysts were detected, which followed the same order as the degradation efficiency of NOR. The highest TOC removal rate of BVP/GQDs/PCN can reach up to 55.8% in 120 min, which is 15.1 times and 3.6 times higher than PCN (3.7%) and BiVO4 (15.6%), respectively, indicating the obviously enhanced mineralization ability of ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalysts for NOR.

The reaction kinetics of NOR degradation were fitted by pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. 4c), which can be expressed as −ln (Ct /C0 ) = kt, where Ct and C0 are the concentration of NOR at a certain irradiation time t and the concentration of NOR at adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the catalysts and NOR, respectively, and k is the apparent rate constant, which is the slope of the liner correlation. It can be seen that the apparent rate constant of BVP/GQDs/PCN is about 36 times and 4 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and BiVO4, respectively. Compared with ever reported similar photocatalysts, BVP/GQDs/PCN exhibited excellent performance in visible-light-induced photodegradation of NOR (Table S2).

The stability and reusability of BVP/GQDs/PCN were investigated through a cycling experiment. As shown in Fig. 4d, no obvious loss of photocatalytic performance could be observed after 4 cycling tests. Moreover, the SEM image (Fig. S6a) and XRD pattern (Fig. S6b) of the used catalyst suggested that the catalyst remained intact during the cycling tests. The Bi leaching data of BVP/GQDs/PCN catalyst were determined by ICP-MS of the reaction solution after 120 min reaction [49]. The concentration of leached Bi is 0.4 mg/L. About 0.04% of Bi leaching was detected in the reaction solution of the first cycle. These results indicated that the ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN catalyst exhibited good stability and durability in photodegradation reaction system.

The influence of pH values of the solution was investigated, because it affects the redox potential and kinds of radical species [50,51]. The initial pH of solution is 7.6, which were adjusted to be 4.6 and 9.6 by addition of HNO3 and NaOH. Fig. S7 shows that the NOR degradation rate increases with increasing pH from 4.6 to 9.6. It can be concluded that neutral and weak alkaline circumstances were favorable for the NOR degradation. The piperazine of NOR undergoes amine protonation which shows lower reactivity, when the pH of the solution is lower than 7.6, therefore the degradation efficiency of NOR decreased. Besides, H+ has a scavenging effect on
OH generated in the solution [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Z-scheme interfacial charge transfer mechanism of BVP/GQDs/PCN
ce:section.ce:para: The transient photocurrent responses of the as-synthesized photocatalysts were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the photocurrent of BVP/GQDs/PCN was much higher than that of single PCN and BiVO4, as well as binary BVP/PCN, suggesting the obviously increased light harvesting ability and charge transfer efficiency of ternary photocatalyst, which is consistent with the results of photodegradation experiments.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes can be examined by PL emission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5b, the PL intensities of the as-prepared samples were ranked as: BVP/GQDs/PCN < BVP/PCN < BiVO4 < PCN < g-C3N4. The ternary sample BVP/GQDs/PCN exhibited the lowest PL intensity, suggesting the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. Moreover, compared with binary BVP/PCN, the lower PL intensity of BVP/GQDs/PCN indicated the promoted interfacial charge transfer between BiVO4 and PCN when GQDs was used as solid-state electron mediator. To further prove that, the TR-PL spectra of the samples were collected to determine the lifetime of the photogenerated carriers and the charge transfer dynamics of the photocatalysts. The fluorescence intensity was monitored upon excitation at 375 nm, and the TR-PL spectra of the as‐prepared samples were well fitted to the three-exponential decay model [53]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the average life‐time (τ) of charge carriers in the PCN and BiVO4 were 2.77 and 5.82 ns, respectively. BVP/PCN exhibited a little longer lifetime of excited carriers (5.88 ns) than BiVO4 and PCN, suggesting the formation of heterojunction structure. In particular, BVP/GQDs/PCN showed a notably increased lifetime (8.41 ns) of charge carriers among these samples, which can be attributed to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction using GQDs as an electron mediator. The results of PL and TR-PL characterization of the samples indicated the inhibited recombination of photogenerated carriers and rapid interfacial charge transfer of BVP/GQDs/PCN, which should be beneficial for the improvement of photocatalytic activity [54].

To further evaluate the interfacial charge transfer of the photocatalysts, EIS Nyquist plots of the samples were recorded (Fig. 5d). The radius of arc is connected with the resistance of interfacial charge transfer, and a larger arc radius means a higher resistance value. It can be found that among these samples, BVP/GQDs/PCN possessed the smallest circular arc radius, that is, it had the smallest resistance of interfacial charge transfer, which further confirmed the promoted interfacial charge transfer of ternary sample across GQDs.

To investigate the charge transfer mechanism of ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst, XPS valence band spectra of g-C3N4, PCN and BiVO4 were recorded (Fig. 6 a). The VB potentials of g-C3N4, PCN and BiVO4 were determined as 1.50, 1.40 and 2.68 V, respectively. According to the DR UV–vis spectra of these samples, the band gap of g-C3N4, PCN and BiVO4 were 2.66, 2.64 and 2.35 V, respectively. The CB positions of g-C3N4, PCN and BiVO4 were estimated to be −1.19, −1.24 and 0.33 V, respectively, based on the empirical formula of Eg = EVB – ECB. Clearly, PCN can retain the enhanced reduction ability of g-C3N4 with a more negative CB position than g-C3N4. The P-doping would induce a downshifting of the CB edge according to the average electronegativity theory [55]. The band gap energy value of BVP is almost the same as BiVO4 [37,38]. Hence, the VB and CB positions of BiVO4 can be used as substitute for those of BVP. Based on these data, the band gap structures of these samples are illustrated in Fig. S8.

The CB and VB potentials of photocatalysts can directly influence the generation of active species in photodegradation process. Therefore, in order to study the charge transfer mechanism of ternary catalysts, the active specie trapping experiments for NOR degradation and ESR detections were carried out. Three typical chemicals, BQ, EDTA-2Na and IPA were used as scavengers of superoxide radical (
O2 −), hole (h +) and
OH, respectively [56]. In addition, an experiment with N2 purging was performed to confirm the role of dissolved oxygen in the generation of
O2 − species. As shown in Fig. 6b, only a slight decrease of NOR degradation efficiency was found with the addition of BQ or under N2 atmosphere, demonstrating that
O2 − played a minor role in the photodegradation of NOR. However, the degradation of NOR was obviously suppressed in the presence of EDTA-2Na and IPA, suggesting that h + and
OH played a crucial role in this photocatalytic system. Taking into account that
OH are mainly generated through the oxidation of H2O by photogenerated holes, the conclusion can be derived that
OH is the dominated active species in photodegradation of NOR on BVP/GQDs/PCN.

The results of DMPO spin-trapping ESR characterization of ternary and binary samples can further confirm the photocatalytic active species. As shown in Fig. 6c, the four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 can be clearly observed on BVP/GQDs/PCN under visible light irradiation, while no signal was detected using BVP/PCN as photocatalyst or in dark. It can be found from Fig. 6d, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 were obtained under visible light irradiation over BVP/GQDs/PCN. Similarly, no signal was observed on BVP/PCN or in dark. It indicated that both
O2 − and
OH can be generated on ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN, but can’t be generated on binary BVP/PCN, suggesting the different charge transfer mechanism on these samples.

On the basis of the above results, a Z-scheme interfacial charge transfer mechanism of ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst was proposed as shown in Scheme 2 . Under visible light irradiation, both PCN and BVP were excited, and electrons and holes were generated. The electrons on the CB of BVP combined with the holes on the VB of PCN via an interfacial charge transfer process, in which GQDs served as a solid-state electron mediator. As a result, an efficient Z-scheme charge transfer was achieved, resulting in an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Because the CB potential of PCN is more negative than the redox potential of O2/
O2 − (ca. −0.33 V vs NHE), the photogenerated electrons in the CB of PCN can reduce the adsorbed O2 to generate
O2 −. In the meantime, the VB potential of BVP is more positive than the redox potential of
OH/OH− (ca. +2.40 V vs NHE), leading to the formation of
OH through the oxidation of H2O by photogenerated holes. The Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism of ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN provided a higher redox ability of photogenerated holes and electrons.

While, without GQDs, the binary BVP/PCN showed a typical type-II heterojunction charge transfer mechanism. The photogenerated electrons transferred from the CB of PCN to that of BVP, and the photogenerated holes moved from the VB of BVP to that of PCN. Due to the insufficient redox ability, the holes on the VB of PCN cannot react with H2O to generate
OH and the electrons on the CB of BVP also cannot reduce the adsorbed O2 to
O2 −. Therefore, the binary BVP/PCN photocatalyst exhibited the inhibited activity in NOR photodegradation. Based on the above discussion, the role of GQDs in ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst can be concluded into three points: promoting interfacial charge transfer between BVP and PCN as an electron mediator, suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and extending the range of visible light absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity evaluation and degradation pathway of NOR
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the incomplete mineralization of antibiotics might lead to the rise of acute biological toxicity due to the formation of highly toxic degradation intermediates [57]. Therefore, the toxicity evaluation of reaction solutions was carried out by bioluminescence inhibition assay. A larger INH indicates a higher toxicity to V. fischeri. As shown in Fig. 7 , the original INH of NOR solution to V. fischeri was 36.3%. After 20 min visible light irradiation, the INH of reaction solutions all increased over BVP/GQDs/PCN, BVP/PCN, and PCN, implying the generation of highly toxic intermediates. In particular, when BVP/PCN and PCN were used as photocatalysts, the INH of reaction solutions rapidly increased to 68.3% and 62.5%, respectively, after 20 min reaction. And the acute toxicity of the reaction solutions maintained at a high level during the whole photodegradation reaction of 120 min. However, for ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst, the INH of reaction solutions gradually increased to the highest level (59.3%) till 80 min, and then fast decreased to 31.3%, which was lower than that of original NOR solution. The different detoxication performance of ternary photocatalyst with binary or single counterparts could be attributed to their different charge transfer mechanism. The Z-scheme BVP/GQDs/PCN photocatalyst possessed higher photocatalytic redox capacity compared with binary BVP/PCN with type-Ⅱ heterojunction. The different active species in these photocatalytic systems would lead to different degradation pathways of NOR.

The reaction solutions were analyzed by HPLC-MS and some probable degradation intermediates of NOR were identified as shown in Fig. S9. According to the HPLC-MS analysis and related literatures [58,59], three possible degradation pathways (P1-P3) were proposed (Fig. 8 ), including piperazinyl ring cleavage (P1), decarboxylation (P2) and hydroxylation (P3). In P1, NOR could be transformed to N1 (m/z 318) by replacement of F with
OH on benzene ring [8,60]. Then, N2 and N3 were generated from N1 through piperazine ring cleavage and modified at para quinolone group, respectively. N3 would be continuously attacked by
OH to form N4, which had higher toxicity than NOR [10]. P2 is the ring cleavage of piperazine. The benzene ring of the piperazine in NOR is easily attacked by free radicals due to its higher electron density [61]. Therefore, the piperazine ring of NOR was firstly attacked by
OH to generate N6 (m/z 350), which was further decarbonylated to form N7 (m/z 294) [2], and subsequently transformed to N8 (m/z 251) and N9 (m/z 236) through the deamination process. As for P3, NOR was directly transformed into N11 (m/z 292) by decarbonylation, which was dominated by direct photodecomposition. Subsequently, N11 was further transformed into N12 (m/z 276) by dehydroxylation, which also had higher toxicity than NOR [62].

Three possible degradation pathways of NOR should simultaneously take place on BVP/GQDs/PCN because of the existence of multiple active species, including
OH,
O2 −, and h +. As the light irradiation time was prolonged, the toxic intermediates N3, N4, N11 and N12 were generated, then totally degraded at 120 min (as shown in Fig. S9). However, because
OH cannot be generated on BVP/PCN and PCN, NOR degradation should mainly occur through decarboxylation pathway (P3). The rapidly increasing of acute toxicity of the reaction solutions should be attributed to the dominated generation and accumulation of highly toxic N11 and N12 in the reaction solutions, which has been confirmed by HPLC-MS results (Fig. S10). As a result, the Z-scheme ternary BVP/GQDs/PCN heterojunctions exhibited notably enhanced detoxication performance in NOR degradation compared with pristine PCN and binary BVP/PCN with type-II heterojunction.

Ternary Z-scheme heterojunction BVP/GQDs/PCN were fabricated for visible-light-induced photodegradation of NOR. By coupling the highly dispersed GQDs and BVP nanoplates with PCN nanosheets, the obtained photocatalysts exhibited promoted light harvesting capacity and separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. GQDs worked as the electron mediator for suppressing the recombination of photogenerated electrons–holes, promoting interfacial charge transfer between BVP and PCN, and broadening the range of visible light response. Consequently, BVP/GQDs/PCN exhibited superior photocatalytic activity (86.3%) for NOR degradation under visible light irradiation compared with the single and binary counterparts. In particular, benefiting from the higher redox ability and the generation of multiple active species of Z-scheme photocatalytic system, notably enhanced mineralization ability and detoxication performance were achieved on BVP/GQDs/PCN. This work provides a good example to promote the detoxication performance in the photodegradation of antibiotics by regulating the interfacial charge transfer mechanism and the degradation pathway of antibiotics.
